source_title: Teaching Data Journalism: A Systematic Review
author: various

extracts.extract: systematic reviews in social sciences are called for when a general overall picture of the evidence in a topic area is needed to direct future research efforts
extracts.relevance_rationale: The need for systematic evidence synthesis to direct research efforts is a methodological principle independent of AI tools

extracts.extract: Traditional literature reviews, unlike systematic ones, lack rigorous, transparent and replicable explicit methodology. Systematic reviews are quite important in helping researchers to make sense of the ever-growing research literature on any particular topic. If the researchers choose to work based on the findings or evidence shared by a single case study, they run the risk of getting biased.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The distinction between rigorous systematic reviews and ad-hoc literature reviews remains methodologically fundamental

extracts.extract: we searched Google Scholar, Web of Science, Scopus, Science Direct, JSTOR & ERIC databases using the specific set of keywords as exact phrases and using the Boolean operator OR. The search query was: teaching data journalism OR data journalism education OR data journalism training.
extracts.relevance_rationale: LLMs can now assist with database searching and keyword expansion, though the systematic protocol design itself endures

extracts.extract: In the second phase, studies citing these selected studies (as per Google Scholar indexing of citations) were examined separately and relevant studies which fell within the aforementioned criteria were selected. To make the selection of corpus for the review comprehensive and systematic, the reference list of the selected studies was examined to find any relevant study which was not yet part of the corpus.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Citation chaining and snowball searches can now be partially automated by AI tools like Semantic Scholar and Elicit, though human judgment on relevance remains

extracts.extract: Thomas and Harden (2008) suggest a three-step approach to carry out the thematic analysis for systematic reviews of qualitative studies. In the first stage, the literature corpus is coded through a line-by-line reading. These codes are used for formulating descriptive themes and analytical themes at later stages.
extracts.relevance_rationale: LLMs can now assist with line-by-line coding and theme formulation, though human interpretive judgment for analytical themes endures

extracts.extract: a content matrix was created in tabular form where each column was devoted to the broader themes in the RQs (like challenges, methods of teaching, etc.) and also other bibliographic data for each publication. Coders placed the excerpts from the individual studies in the literature corpus in the relevant columns of the matrix, effectively isolating the relevant excerpts according to themes.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Content matrix creation and excerpt extraction can be accelerated by LLMs, though the thematic framework design requires human judgment

extracts.extract: translation is the process of taking concepts from one study and recognising the same concepts in another study, though they may not be expressed using identical words. After this stage, the concepts and findings from different studies were summarised according to their similarities and differences to synthesise the findings of the review.
extracts.relevance_rationale: LLMs excel at concept translation across studies using different terminology, though human validation of equivalence claims is still needed

extracts.extract: data journalism training is an emerging field which demands an ensemble of skillsets and expertise to complete the workflow, especially in case of advanced projects. This aspect has raised a challenge in formulating an exact boundary to ascertain what skillsets to focus on and which tools to be included in the training.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The challenge of defining DJ skill boundaries is intensified by LLMs which blur the line between journalist and analyst capabilities

extracts.extract: use of project-based approach is a common method in data journalism teaching, with slighter variations in its implementation. This approach follows a teaching hospital method by engaging students to work on a data story project, with each of them ideally going through different steps in the production of a data story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Project-based learning and teaching-hospital pedagogy remain effective regardless of whether LLMs assist with individual pipeline steps

extracts.extract: a major recommendation is to integrate lessons on basic numeracy into existing units of the programme, especially during the first year. Collaborative learning and hands-on practical sessions are also recommended as confidence-building measures by several scholars.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Basic numeracy integration endures but LLMs can now scaffold confidence-building with on-demand statistical explanations

extracts.extract: Most of the studies which were examined in the review consider data journalism education primarily as a means of imparting technological and management skills required to deal with datasets, rather than developing critical data literacy in students. data literacy is overly perceived as technical abilities like extracting data, making statistical analysis, creating visualizations, interpreting and reporting appropriately.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The critique that DJ education overemphasizes technical skills at the expense of critical data literacy is amplified, not resolved, by LLM availability

extracts.extract: there is a widespread tendency in many studies to consider data or datasets as passive, value-free elements which are used by students to develop technological skills. This denial of the materiality of datasets as techno-social assemblages is problematic, since such an approach stops short of helping students to explore the role played by the techno-social data infrastructures which formulated the dataset and their influence on the meaning-making process.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Recognizing datasets as techno-social assemblages shaped by power and infrastructure is a critical literacy that becomes more important with AI-mediated data

extracts.extract: ethical aspects of data journalism were largely absent in the course materials reviewed by them and that data journalism educators lacked awareness about ethical data journalism practices. lessons about ethical practices are necessary in data journalism training as these enable students to understand the importance of accountability, transparency and privacy issues.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ethics education in DJ including accountability, transparency and privacy becomes more urgent as AI tools introduce new ethical risks

extracts.extract: One major challenge with systematic reviews in social science disciplines like journalism studies is the existence of considerable amount of grey literature. Formulating a search protocol to systematically include relevant grey literature available in the form of industry reports or non-peer-reviewed research reports brought out by academic institutions was beyond the scope of this study.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The challenge of grey literature in systematic reviews persists and is arguably compounded by proliferating AI-generated content
